before: Assistant: Short cycles (for example 4–8 weeks) with a short grace period (3–7 days), repayment dates tied to each group’s income rhythm (paydays, market/harvest days, stipend schedules), and social incentives—priority access to the next round, small public recognition, or modest fee waivers—tend to boost on-time repayment. Do the three borrower groups differ in size, income cadence, or regular community events so I can recommend specific cycle lengths and exact repayment dates?
after: Assistant: I can still help with the current question without relying on earlier restricted details.

before: Assistant: I can draft the core contract clauses to protect your PIA templates/heuristics and enforce a restricted-license model. Suggested provisions: - IP: define Background vs. Foreground IP; specify that deliverables are either assigned to your company or licensed under a narrow, exclusive (or non‑exclusive) restricted license; include a clear prohibition on commercialization or republishing without express consent; add license‑back terms only if needed for limited partner reuse. - Licensing scope & downstream rights: purpose‑limited use, no sublicensing without written consent, explicit rules for attribution, and limits on downstream sublicenses or transfers. - Confidentiality/NDA: non‑use, non‑disclosure, obligations to return/destroy materials on termination, and limited carveouts for compelled disclosure with notice. - Data & privacy (DPA): obligations for handling any process maps or personal data, minimum security standards (e.g., encryption, access controls), breach-notification timeline (e.g., 72 hours), and limits on retention/replication of sensitive diagrams. - Subcontracting: conditions for partner subprocessors/subcontractors, flow‑down obligations, and your right to approve critical subcontractors. - Audit & compliance: right to audit with defined frequency and scope, remediation steps, and consequences for non‑compliance. - Deliverables, acceptance & change control: defined deliverable list, acceptance criteria, change‑request process, and versioning controls for templates/heuristics. - Warranties & representations: partner warrants no third‑party claims, proper authority, and compliance with applicable laws; representations about not misusing or commercializing the materials. - Indemnity & limitations: remedies for IP/confidentiality breaches, injunctive relief language, and proportionate liability caps. - Term, termination & post‑termination: termination for breach, post‑termination return/destruction, and whether limited continued license rights survive (e.g., for previously delivered, accepted materials). - Fees & remedies: payment milestones, escrow for critical material (if desirable), and interim injunctive remedies for misuse. Quick questions to tailor the draft: 1. Do you want assignment of deliverables to your company or an exclusive restricted license? 2. Which governing law/jurisdiction should the contract use? 3. Will the partner process any personal data (yes/no), and do you expect subcontractors to be used? Tell me those three choices and whether you want a short clause set (concise, ~2–4 pages) or a full contract; I’ll draft the clause set accordingly.
after: Assistant: I can draft a focused clause set to protect your PIA templates/heuristics and implement a restricted‑license model. Core provisions I’d include: - IP: distinguish Background vs. Foreground IP; require either assignment of deliverables to your company or grant of a narrowly tailored exclusive/restricted license; expressly prohibit commercialization, Republishing, or redistribution without written consent; limited license‑back only if explicitly needed. - License scope & downstream rights: purpose‑limited use, no sublicensing or transfer without prior written approval, attribution rules, and explicit limits on downstream reuse. - Confidentiality/NDA: non‑use/non‑disclosure obligations, handling of derivative materials, return/destruction on termination, and a notice requirement for compelled disclosures. - Data & privacy (DPA) if applicable: obligations for handling any process maps or personal data, minimum security controls (encryption, access controls), retention limits, and breach‑notification timing (e.g., 72 hours). - Subcontracting: restrictions on subcontractors, required flow‑down clauses, and your right to approve critical subprocessors. - Audit & compliance: audit rights, remediation steps, and consequences for non‑compliance (including remediation timelines). - Deliverables, acceptance & change control: clear deliverable list, acceptance criteria, versioning and change‑request procedure, and rules for maintaining template integrity. - Warranties & representations: partner warrants ownership/authority and that materials won’t infringe or be commercialized; promise not to repurpose heuristics. - Indemnity & remedies: remedies for IP/confidentiality breaches, injunctive relief language, and proportional liability/limits as appropriate. - Term, termination & post‑termination: termination for breach, return/destruction obligations, and whether any limited license survives for already‑delivered materials. - Fees, escrow & interim relief: payment milestones, optional escrow for critical source files, and rights to seek interim injunctive relief for misuse. Quick tailoring questions so I can draft the right document: 1) Do you prefer assignment of deliverables to your company, or an exclusive restricted license (choose one)? 2) Which governing law/jurisdiction should apply? 3) Will the partner process any personal data? (yes/no) Do you expect subcontractors to be used? (yes/no) 4) Do you want a short clause set (concise, ~2–4 pages) or a full contract? Give me those choices and I’ll produce the draft.

line_after: User: Temporarily, do not rely on my past messages.

before: Assistant: Good — to make those clauses durable across informal co‑ops, include the following essentials: - Clear definition of revenue pools: what money counts (sales, donations, grants, in‑kind), how pooled funds are tracked, and which income streams are excluded. - Allocation method: precise formulas or priority order (percentages, tiers, fixed fees), timing of distributions, and rounding/tolerance rules. - Record‑keeping & transparency: required books/ledgers, who has access, regular reporting cadence, and simple audit rights. - Payment mechanics: who distributes funds, bank/payment details, reconciliation process, and fallback if the payor is unavailable. - Membership changes: how shares shift when members join/leave, treatment of departing members’ accrued but unpaid amounts, and transfer restrictions. - Low‑burden dispute path: staged escalation (informal meet → mediation → binding small‑claims/arbitration), short deadlines for each stage, and an interim-preservation step (e.g., escrow or limited injunctive relief). - Conflict‑avoidance tools: clear decision thresholds for commercial choices, quorum/consent rules, and simple conflict‑of‑interest disclosures. - Amendment & exit: streamlined amendment procedure for minor changes and a clear exit/termination process for winding up revenue allocations. - Enforcement & remedies: permissible remedies for breach (repayment, interest, cost allocation) proportionate to co‑op scale. - Plain‑language summaries and templates: a one‑page member summary and fill‑in templates for receipts, reconciliations, and dispute notices. Pilot the clauses with one co‑op first and collect specific examples of where language caused confusion — that feedback will let you iterate safely. Would you like a plain‑language revenue‑share + staged dispute clause draft to test with the pilot? If so, tell me: typical revenue types to include, how often you want distributions, preferred governing law/currency, and whether the draft should be bilingual.
after: Assistant: - Allocation method: precise formulas or priority order (percentages, tiers, fixed fees), timing of distributions, and rounding/tolerance rules. - Record‑keeping & transparency: required books/ledgers, who has access, regular reporting cadence, and simple audit rights. - Payment mechanics: who distributes funds, bank/payment details, reconciliation process, and fallback if the payor is unavailable. - Membership changes: how shares shift when members join/leave, treatment of departing members’ accrued but unpaid amounts, and transfer restrictions. - Low‑burden dispute path: staged escalation (informal meet → mediation → binding small‑claims/arbitration), short deadlines for each stage, and an interim-preservation step (e.g., escrow or limited injunctive relief). - Conflict‑avoidance tools: clear decision thresholds for commercial choices, quorum/consent rules, and simple conflict‑of‑interest disclosures. - Amendment & exit: streamlined amendment procedure for minor changes and a clear exit/termination process for winding up revenue allocations. - Enforcement & remedies: permissible remedies for breach (repayment, interest, cost allocation) proportionate to co‑op scale. - Plain‑language summaries and templates: a one‑page member summary and fill‑in templates for receipts, reconciliations, and dispute notices. Pilot the clauses with one co‑op first and collect specific examples of where language caused confusion — that feedback will let you iterate safely. Would you like a plain‑language revenue‑share + staged dispute clause draft to test with the pilot? If so, tell me: typical revenue types to include, how often you want distributions, preferred governing law/currency, and whether the draft should be bilingual.

before: Assistant: Make sure the partner agreement includes IP assignment, strict licensing, NDA provisions, and audit rights—shall I draft the core contract clauses?
after: Assistant: I can draft the core contract clauses. Key provisions I’d include: - IP: clear definitions of Background vs. Foreground IP and either an assignment of deliverables to you or an exclusive, perpetual, worldwide license; license-back terms if the partner needs limited reuse. - Licensing scope: narrow, purpose-limited rights, no sublicensing without consent, and restrictions on commercialization of your templates/heuristics. - Confidentiality/NDA: non‑use, non‑disclosure, obliged return/destruction of materials, and carveouts for required disclosures. - Data & privacy: obligations for handling any process maps or personal data, security standards, breach-notification timelines. - Audit & compliance: right to audit with defined frequency, scope, and remediation steps. - Warranties/representations: no third‑party claims, accuracy, and compliance with laws. - Indemnity & limitations: remedies for misuse and appropriate liability caps. - Term, termination, and post‑termination rights: retention/destruction, continued license rights (if any), and transition assistance. - Change control and remedies (injunctive relief for IP/confidentiality breaches). Do you prefer assignment of deliverables to your company or an exclusive license? Also tell me the governing law/jurisdiction to use and whether the partner will process any personal data—I'll draft a concise clause set based on your choices.

after: Assistant: I can draft a focused clause set to protect your PIA templates/heuristics and implement a restricted‑license model. Core provisions I’d include: - IP: distinguish Background vs. Foreground IP; require either assignment of deliverables to your company or a narrowly tailored exclusive/restricted license; prohibit commercialization, republication, or redistribution without prior written consent; limited license‑back only if explicitly needed. - License scope & downstream rights: purpose‑limited use, no sublicensing or transfers without approval, clear attribution rules, and explicit limits on downstream reuse or incorporation into partner products. - Confidentiality/NDA: non‑use/non‑disclosure obligations, handling of derivatives, return/destruction on termination, and notice requirements for compelled disclosures. - Data & privacy (DPA) if applicable: obligations for handling process maps or personal data, minimum security controls (e.g., encryption, access controls), retention limits, and breach‑notification timing (suggested 72 hours). - Subcontracting: restrictions on subcontractors, required flow‑down clauses, and your right to approve critical subprocessors. - Audit & compliance: audit rights, remediation steps, and consequences for non‑compliance with timelines for cure. - Deliverables, acceptance & change control: explicit deliverable list, acceptance criteria, versioning, change‑request procedure, and rules to preserve template integrity. - Warranties & representations: partner warrants ownership/authority to provide materials and that they will not commercialize or repurpose the heuristics. - Indemnity & remedies: remedies for IP/confidentiality breaches, injunctive relief language, and proportional liability/limits as appropriate. - Term, termination & post‑termination: termination for breach, return/destruction obligations, and whether any limited license survives for already‑delivered materials. - Fees, escrow & interim relief: payment milestones, optional escrow for critical source files, and rights to seek interim injunctive relief for misuse. Quick tailoring questions so I can draft the right document: 1) Do you prefer assignment of deliverables to your company, or an exclusive restricted license? (choose one) 2) Which governing law/jurisdiction should apply? 3) Will the partner process any personal data? (yes/no) Do you expect subcontractors to be used? (yes/no) 4) Do you want a short clause set (concise, ~2–4 pages) or a full contract? 5) Would you like an escrow clause included for source files? (yes/no) Give me those choices and I’ll produce the draft.

before: Assistant: Define revenue pools clearly, set a dispute-escalation path, and include a streamlined amendment process — pilot the clauses with one co-op for feedback; would you like a plain-language revenue-share draft to test with them?
after: Assistant: Good — to make those clauses durable across informal co‑ops, include the following essentials: - Clear definition of revenue pools: what money counts (sales, donations, grants, in‑kind), how pooled funds are tracked, and which income streams are excluded. - Allocation method: precise formulas or priority order (percentages, tiers, fixed fees), timing of distributions, and rounding/tolerance rules. - Record‑keeping & transparency: required books/ledgers, who has access, regular reporting cadence, and simple audit rights. - Payment mechanics: who distributes funds, bank/payment details, reconciliation process, and fallback if the payor is unavailable. - Membership changes: how shares shift when members join/leave, treatment of departing members’ accrued but unpaid amounts, and transfer restrictions. - Low‑burden dispute path: staged escalation (informal meet → mediation → binding small‑claims/arbitration), short deadlines for each stage, and an interim-preservation step (e.g., escrow or limited injunctive relief). - Conflict‑avoidance tools: clear decision thresholds for commercial choices, quorum/consent rules, and simple conflict‑of‑interest disclosures. - Amendment & exit: streamlined amendment procedure for minor changes and a clear exit/termination process for winding up revenue allocations. - Enforcement & remedies: permissible remedies for breach (repayment, interest, cost allocation) proportionate to co‑op scale. - Plain‑language summaries and templates: a one‑page member summary and fill‑in templates for receipts, reconciliations, and dispute notices. Pilot the clauses with one co‑op first and collect specific examples of where language caused confusion — that feedback will let you iterate safely. Would you like a plain‑language revenue‑share + staged dispute clause draft to test with the pilot? If so, tell me: typical revenue types to include, how often you want distributions, preferred governing law/currency, and whether the draft should be bilingual.

after: Assistant: - Allocation method: define exact formulas or priority order (percentages, tiers, fixed fees), timing of distributions, handling of fractions/rounding, and rules for withheld or disputed amounts. - Record‑keeping & transparency: required books/ledgers, who may access records, frequency of reporting, simple reconciliation steps, and lightweight audit rights. - Payment mechanics: designated payor/distributor, bank/payment details and authorization, reconciliation process, fallback if the payor is unavailable, and timelines for clearing. - Membership changes: how shares adjust when members join or leave, treatment of accrued-but-unpaid amounts on exit, transfer restrictions, and onboarding/offboarding paperwork. - Low‑burden dispute path: staged escalation (informal meeting → internal review → mediation → binding small‑claims/arbitration as needed), short deadlines for each stage, and an interim‑preservation mechanism (escrow, limited injunctive relief, or temporary hold on distributions). - Conflict‑avoidance tools: clear decision thresholds for commercial choices, quorum/consent rules for major vs. routine decisions, and simple conflict‑of‑interest disclosure and recusal steps. - Amendment & exit: streamlined amendment procedure for minor updates, clear thresholds for major amendments, and an exit/winding‑up process that specifies priority of payments. - Enforcement & remedies: proportionate remedies (repayment, interest, cost allocation), caps appropriate to co‑op scale, and an option for interim relief where necessary. - Plain‑language summaries & templates: one‑page member summaries, fill‑in templates for receipts and reconciliations, and a short dispute‑notice form to keep the process low‑friction. Pilot the clauses with one co‑op and collect real examples of confusion so you can iterate the language. If you want, I can draft a plain‑language revenue‑share + staged dispute clause for the pilot. Tell me: 1) Typical revenue types to include (sales, grants, donations, service fees, royalties, etc.). 2) How often you want distributions (weekly, monthly, quarterly). 3) Preferred governing law and currency. 4) Whether the draft should be bilingual.

before: Assistant: Compact residency-application checklist 1. Project title and one-line elevator summary 2. 250–400 word project summary (aims, scope, significance) 3. Clear research questions or objectives 4. Specific access needs (collections, archives, equipment, negotiated permissions) 5. Methodology and day-to-day plan while in residence 6. Expected outputs (papers, exhibitions, catalogues, public events) and delivery timeline 7. Proposed residency timeline and how you’ll use the residency period (weekly milestones) 8. Brief budget or resource request (materials, travel, housing, conservation handling fees) 9. CV (2-page highlights version) and 2–3 relevant work samples or links 10. Letters of support / named collaborators or institutional partners (if available) 11. Ethical or conservation considerations and any required approvals/insurance 12. Digital access needs and data-management plan (if applicable) 13. Contact information and emergency/host liaison details 14.
